     1	# Skill — testing
     2	
     3	## Tests prove the edited contract
     4	
     5	Choose tests that witness the behavior or invariant changed. Prefer small deterministic checks near the contract over broad smoke tests that can pass while the edited rule is broken.
     6	
     7	## Nix is the normal test gate
     8	
     9	Expose durable project checks through the flake. Pure checks belong in flake checks. Test-only binaries use clear test names and do not become runtime services.
    10	
    11	Run the narrow check that proves the edit, then the broader gate required by the repo before commit. Build that single check directly (`nix build .#checks.<system>.<check>`); reserve full `nix flake check` for pre-commit, and run whole-engine sweeps dry-run first with background builds and logs on disk. Record exact failures when a check cannot run.
    12	
    13	Do not substitute an unrelated passing check for the one that proves the edited surface.
    14	
    15	Do not pipe a test or check run through `tail` or `head` when judging pass/fail — the pipe substitutes the pager's exit code for the run's own and can truncate per-binary results into a false green. Capture full output to a file and read the run's real exit status (or `${PIPESTATUS[0]}` when a pipe is unavoidable); judge pass/fail from that exit status, not a piped tail.
    16	
    17	## Keep state explicit
    18	
    19	Stateful tests name their resources, host requirements, cleanup, and failure artifacts. They do not depend on hidden local state. If a test needs credentials or hardware, mark the requirement and provide a safe skip or manual gate. Place a daemon-sandbox unix socket under a short run root (e.g. `/tmp/<lane>/`) so its path stays under the SUN_LEN limit; never nest the socket under the deep session scratchpad path.
    20	
    21	## Test architecture, not just regression
    22	
    23	When the change protects architecture, write tests for forbidden dependencies, protocol compatibility, ownership boundaries, and invariant preservation. A compile-fail or metadata check can be the right test when runtime behavior is not the contract.
    24	
    25	## Put tests in owned locations
    26	
    27	Keep unit tests close to small pure logic. Put integration tests at crate or component boundaries. Keep generated-surface tests at the generator source, not by patching emitted output.
    28	
    29	## Name tests by behavior
    30	
    31	Test names state the behavior under proof. Avoid names that only repeat the function name or issue number.
    32	
    33	## Failure output is part of the interface
    34	
    35	Assertions should say what invariant failed and show the relevant values. Do not dump secrets or large unrelated state.
    36	
    37	## Keep fixtures minimal
    38	
    39	Fixtures carry only the state needed to prove the case. Prefer named builders or typed records over copied blobs. Internal test, eval, and diagnostic artifacts use typed Rust records with NOTA projection; non-NOTA text fixtures name the external consumer or protocol that requires that exact format. When a fixture encodes compatibility, state the compatibility boundary in the test name or nearby assertion.
    40	
    41	## Do not weaken tests to pass
    42	
    43	If a test fails because the contract changed, update the contract and test together. If it fails because the implementation is wrong, fix the implementation. Do not delete coverage without replacing the proof.
===modules/nix-usage/full.md
     1	# Skill — Nix usage
     2	
     3	## Rules
     4	
     5	Ask Nix what Nix knows. Inspect source, evaluate options, or query the daemon; do not search the store for configuration.
     6	
     7	Use local checkout evaluations for diagnosis. Use a fetched or pinned installable when the evidence must prove another machine can reproduce the build. When a flake wrapper locks the dependency under test, use `--override-input <name> <local-path>` for quick local checks.
     8	
     9	Keep store paths in variables. Do not paste raw store paths into chat, commits, skills, or docs; hashes drift and freeze stale state into prose.
    10	
    11	When a command resolves through the Nix store, a profile, Home Manager output, or generated runtime output, use that as evidence and trace the owning source. Do not patch the effective command by replacing managed links, adding PATH shims, editing copied installed source, or adding ad hoc dependency symlinks.
    12	
    13	## Command shapes
    14	
    15	Inspect daemon-visible settings narrowly:
    16	
    17	```sh
    18	nix config show | rg '^(builders|builders-use-substitutes|max-jobs|substituters|trusted-public-keys|trusted-users)'
    19	```
    20	
    21	Build and keep the output path transient:
    22	
    23	```sh
    24	result=$(nix build <installable> --no-link --print-out-paths)
    25	ls "$result"
    26	```
    27	
    28	Dry-run before a heavy build to read the miss/rebuild surface and catch remote-builder degradation early:
    29	
    30	```sh
    31	nix build <installable> --dry-run
    32	```
    33	
    34	Use a remote-builder smoke test only when that path is the claim. Force local slots to zero so the command fails instead of silently building locally:
    35	
    36	```sh
    37	nix build <installable> --no-link --print-build-logs   --option max-jobs 0   --print-out-paths
    38	```
    39	
    40	Use an uncached small derivation or `--rebuild` when substitutes would hide the builder path.
    41	
    42	## Evidence
    43	
    44	- `nix build` proves the build result or closure.
    45	- `nix flake check` proves the repo's pure checks.
    46	- `nix run .#<app>` proves a repo-exposed runner or one-shot tool.
    47	- `nix run nixpkgs#<package> -- <arguments>` supplies a missing tool without mutating the environment.
    48	
    49	Name the exact installable and whether the result is local diagnosis, fetched-build evidence, or remote-builder evidence.
===modules/nix-discipline/full.md
     1	# Skill — Nix discipline
     2	
     3	## Model services as NixOS modules
     4	
     5	Long-running services are NixOS modules with typed options, systemd units, users, files, and firewall policy. Do not smuggle service shape into ad-hoc shell scripts or container-only definitions when the host is NixOS.
     6	
     7	## Choose flake inputs deliberately
     8	
     9	Use registry or remote inputs for shared dependencies. Do not commit local file inputs. For temporary integration checks, override inputs at command time instead of changing the committed flake.
    10	
    11	Keep lock-file updates intentional and reviewable. Pin through the lock file, not duplicate hashes in `flake.nix`.
    12	
    13	## Build and deploy from reproducible inputs
    14	
    15	Commands that prove a change should build from the committed flake state or an explicit temporary override. Do not rely on an untracked checkout to be present on the target host.
    16	
    17	Compiled artifacts are build outputs. Do not compile at service start. Runtime scripts may select, configure, and launch artifacts; they do not build them.
    18	
    19	Command resolution, Home Manager profiles, package profiles, and runtime outputs are owned by source plus activation. Change them through flake inputs, modules, packages, checks, and deployment, not by PATH shadowing, replacing managed symlinks, mutable profile edits, ad hoc dependency symlinks, or copied store/profile output.
    20	
    21	## Cargo dependencies in crane flakes
    22	
    23	For Rust git dependencies, keep the dependency identity in Cargo metadata and let the lock file carry the revision. Do not add manual output hashes to flake code as the normal fix.
    24	
    25	## Do not bake store paths into source
    26	
    27	Source and docs do not depend on raw store paths. Refer to packages, derivations, options, and outputs by name. Inspect store paths with Nix commands when needed; do not search the store filesystem.
    28	
    29	## Use Nix to get tools
    30	
    31	If a tool is missing, run it through Nix or add it to the dev shell. Do not install ad-hoc host packages to make a build pass.
    32	
    33	## Checks are the gate
    34	
    35	`nix flake check` is the default pre-commit proof for Nix changes. Add narrower package, module, or VM checks when they prove the edited surface better. Stateful deployment checks must name the host, target, and rollback plan.
    36	
    37	## Keep evaluation separate from activation
    38	
    39	Evaluation and build checks prove the derivation graph. Activation and deployment checks prove host behavior. Do not treat a successful build as evidence that a service migrated safely.
    40	
    41	## Module shape
    42	
    43	Keep options typed and documented. Put defaults near option definitions. Keep assertions close to the invariant. Prefer small modules with clear imports over one large conditional module.
    44	
    45	## Prefer data over shell
    46	
    47	Use Nix values, options, derivations, and systemd fields for structure. Shell belongs at the edge where a program must be invoked, and it stays small enough to audit.
===modules/code-implementation/full.md
     1	# Skill - code implementation
     2	
     3	## Rules
     4	
     5	Read the repository instructions, intent, architecture, and touched code path before editing. Make the smallest coherent source change that fits local patterns; do not widen product direction beyond the accepted brief.
     6	
     7	Prefer existing language, framework, schema, and helper shapes. Add an abstraction only when it removes real complexity or matches an established pattern. Use full English names, typed domain objects, enum or record variants instead of boolean control flags, and methods on the data-bearing type that owns behavior.
     8	
     9	A forced special case is not a routine implementation detail. Try to reshape it into the normal case; if the accepted constraints still force an exception, stop and report the design choice instead of burying it.
    10	
    11	When behavior changes a public contract, storage schema, wire format, generated surface, deployment slot, or operations workflow, update the relevant version or state why none is needed. Preserve compatibility unless the brief accepts a break.
    12	
    13	Run the narrowest meaningful check first, then broader checks when shared behavior, generator output, or public interfaces changed. Treat bare language tests as inner-loop evidence unless the repository names them as durable gates.
    14	
    15	Close out file edits by committing and pushing those changes before final output. This is unconditional. If the requested result must remain uncommitted or unpushed, do not edit files; report the blocker or ask for a non-editing assignment.
    16	
    17	If the change creates or consumes a producer dependency, make that dependency portable before closeout. Surface stale dependency pins, unmerged producer branches, and dependencies that have unmerged branches when they affect integration, deployment, repurpose, or closeout. If portable closeout is not possible, report it as a hard blocker.
===modules/code-implementation-core/full.md
     1	# Module - code implementation core
     2	
     3	## Implementation Core Purpose
     4	
     5	Ordinary implementation turns an accepted brief into the smallest coherent
     6	source change that fits the repository. The worker owns local understanding,
     7	code edits, and verification evidence; broader product direction stays with the
     8	brief or the psyche.
     9	
    10	## Implementation Local Fit
    11	
    12	Read repository instructions, intent, architecture, and the touched code path
    13	before editing. Prefer existing language, framework, schema, and helper
    14	patterns. Add an abstraction only when it removes real complexity or matches an
    15	established local pattern.
    16	
    17	Use full English names and typed domain objects. Avoid boolean control flags
    18	where a closed record or enum can name the variants. Put behavior on the
    19	data-bearing type that owns it. Where two enums meet, name the contact point
    20	instead of scattering conditionals.
    21	
    22	Beauty is a correctness gate: a special case should dissolve into the normal
    23	case. If a fix works only by adding a side path that future agents must
    24	remember, keep looking for the shape that makes the rule explicit. If accepted
    25	constraints appear to force that side path, stop and report the forced special
    26	case instead of burying it.
    27	
    28	Patch source repositories, not installed effective state. A Nix store path,
    29	profile, Home Manager output, generated runtime output, or copied installed
    30	source is managed-output evidence, not permission to mutate it. When the durable
    31	source owner is known, an ordinary launcher or profile path is not a blocker:
    32	change source and verify after the normal deployment. Investigate ownership only
    33	when it is unknown or deployment or verification fails. Closeout is blocked when
    34	behavior depends on uncommitted runtime edits, PATH shims, replaced managed
    35	symlinks, or copied installed source.
    36	
    37	## Routine Maintenance
    38	
    39	For a clearly authorized routine maintenance request with known repositories and standard interfaces, one implementation worker follows the direct path: update, build, deploy when requested, and verify. Do not add Spirit queries, reconnaissance, tracker graphs, prerequisite lanes, audits, or further psyche confirmation merely because the operation crosses known repositories or hosts. Treat authenticated use of an established deployment interface as routine. Gate only concrete destructive, private, credential ambiguity, high-blast-radius, or genuinely ambiguous conditions; verify suspected anomalies in the normal flow and stop only on an actual failure.
    40	
    41	Keep routine work within its expected small time and tool bound. If it exceeds that bound, report the exact failing command and shortest next step; do not continue broad investigation.
    42	
    43	## Implementation Version Compatibility
    44	
    45	When behavior changes a public contract, storage schema, wire format, generated
    46	surface, deployment slot, or operations workflow, update the relevant version or
    47	state why none is needed. Preserve compatibility unless the brief explicitly
    48	accepts a break.
    49	
    50	## Implementation Verification
    51	
    52	Run the narrowest meaningful check first, then broader checks when shared
    53	behavior, generator output, or public interfaces changed. In this workspace,
    54	durable test evidence is owned by Nix when the repo exposes it: flake checks,
    55	named check derivations, or named stateful runners. Bare language test commands
    56	are inner-loop evidence unless the repo says otherwise.
    57	
    58	## Implementation Dependency Portability
    59	
    60	If the change creates or consumes a producer dependency, make that dependency
    61	portable before closeout. Surface stale dependency pins, unmerged producer
    62	branches, and dependencies that have unmerged branches when they affect
    63	integration, deployment, repurpose, or closeout. If portable closeout is not
    64	possible, report it as a hard blocker.
===modules/rust-methods/full.md
     1	# Skill — Rust methods and types
     2	
     3	## Methods live on data-bearing types
     4	
     5	Every production Rust function is a method or associated function on a non-zero-sized data-bearing type, or a trait implementation. The exceptions are `fn main()` and test-only helpers. Module-level production `fn`, `const fn`, and `async fn` are forbidden.
     6	
     7	Private helpers follow the same rule. If a helper reads or writes a value, the value or its owner is the receiver. If several calls share state, name the state-bearing object and put the methods there. Prefer `From`, `TryFrom`, and domain traits over standalone projection functions.
     8	
     9	## Schema objects are method surfaces
    10	
    11	Schema-authored records and enums are the nouns. Generated Rust types receive inherent methods or trait implementations; handwritten mirror types and free wrapper functions are drift.
    12	
    13	When generated types lack the right surface:
    14	
    15	1. adjust the schema if the noun is wrong;
    16	2. regenerate;
    17	3. attach behavior to the emitted type or to the runtime owner that carries state.
    18	
    19	Signal, mail, operation, reply, codec, and store-record behavior belongs on those generated objects or on runtime owners such as engines, stores, mailboxes, or ledgers.
    20	
    21	## Async flow is object flow
    22	
    23	Async work does not justify procedural glue. Name each lifecycle object and put behavior on the phase owner: input, sent event, in-flight mail, executor input, state transition, processed reply, or the actor that owns the state. A free `route_*`, `process_*`, `dispatch_*`, or `apply_*` function usually names the missing receiver.
    24	
    25	## Zero-sized method holders are forbidden
    26	
    27	A unit struct whose impl only groups functions is a namespace, not a model. Use a zero-sized type only when the zero-sized value itself carries a real type-level capability, marker, policy, or witness. Parser, router, formatter, or manager names must carry the data or resources they operate on.
    28	
    29	## Types carry domain meaning
    30	
    31	Do not pass domain concepts as primitives when a type can name them. Wrap identifiers, ports, paths, names, tokens, sizes, and validated strings in domain types. Parse and validate at the boundary; carry the typed value inside.
    32	
    33	Do not use string prefixes, sentinel strings, or regex checks to recover a type that should already exist. Tests assert typed structure and behavior, not naming accidents.
    34	
    35	## One concept has one type
    36	
    37	Avoid companion types such as `ThingDetails`, `ThingInfo`, or `ThingData` unless they name a distinct concept with different invariants. If the suffix only means "the fields of Thing", fold it into `Thing` or name the real variant.
    38	
    39	The system mints durable identifiers. Agents and tests do not invent identifiers by concatenating strings unless the type contract explicitly defines that encoding.
    40	
    41	## One object in, one object out
    42	
    43	Methods consume one coherent input object and return one coherent output object. Replace long parameter lists and tuple returns with named request and reply types. Options are typed variants, not booleans.
    44	
    45	## Constructors are associated functions
    46	
    47	Construction belongs on the constructed type: `new`, `from_*`, `try_from_*`, or a domain-specific associated function. Builders are data-bearing when they enforce staged construction; otherwise they are another free-function holder.
    48	
    49	## Names encode direction only when it matters
    50	
    51	Use directional names only for real protocol direction or conversion direction: `RequestToReply`, `ClientInput`, `ServerOutput`. Do not encode call order, layer ancestry, or implementation history in type names.
===modules/repository-closeout/full.md
     1	# Skill - repository closeout
     2	
     3	## Rules
     4	
     5	Use repository closeout after implementation and validation evidence exist. Inspect local instructions and status first, preserve unrelated edits, and do not manufacture green evidence for missing checks.
     6	
     7	Use `jj`/Jujutsu for normal version control. Every description-taking command uses an inline message. Before publishing, confirm bookmark reachability, repository status, and that no descriptionless authored commit is being pushed. Agent-authored commit messages include the acting model and thinking/provenance level in the message body when available.
     8	
     9	After validation, commit and push implementation changes. Do not leave edited work uncommitted or unpushed. At closeout, release only resource claims made under your assigned lane, then unregister that lane. Do not release generic names or another worker's lane.
    10	
    11	If the work creates or consumes a producer dependency, make that dependency portable before publishing. Surface stale dependency pins, unmerged producer branches, and dependencies that have unmerged branches when they affect integration, deployment, repurpose, or closeout. If portable closeout is not possible, report it as a hard blocker.
    12	
    13	For primary-style direct main closeout:
    14	
    15	```sh
    16	jj status --no-pager
    17	jj commit -m 'short imperative message'
    18	jj bookmark set main -r @-
    19	jj git push --bookmark main
    20	```
    21	
    22	Use BEADS/beads for tracked work that must survive the session or coordinate with other work. Close a bead only after acceptance criteria pass or the bead is invalidated; closing notes name durable evidence such as the commit, validation artifact, output file, or superseding task.
    23	
    24	After pushing, verify status is clean or contains only named unrelated files. Report basis commit, bookmark, commands run, push result, and remaining blockers or disposition.
===modules/version-control/full.md
     1	# Skill — version control
     2	
     3	## Use Jujutsu, not raw git
     4	
     5	Use `jj` for version control. Raw `git` is only an escape hatch named in this skill. Every description-taking command uses an inline message or equivalent headless flag; never let a command open an editor.
     6	
     7	## Target the repository explicitly
     8	
     9	When scripting jj against any repository other than the lane's own verified working directory — especially throwaway, demo, or freshly cloned repos — pass an explicit `-R <path>`/`--repository <path>` on every command, or guard the `cd` so its failure hard-stops the script (`cd <path> || exit`). Agent bash resets cwd to the primary workspace each call, so a silently failed `cd` lets cwd fallback aim a mutating command at primary. Never let cwd fallback decide which repository a mutating command hits.
    10	
    11	## Primary workspace stays on main
    12	
    13	In primary, work directly on `main`. Do not create branches. Commit the complete working copy, then move `main` to the committed parent and push.
    14	
    15	```sh
    16	jj status --no-pager
    17	jj commit -m 'short imperative message'
    18	jj bookmark set main -r @-
    19	jj git push --bookmark main
    20	```
    21	
    22	If unrelated dirty files exist, name them and avoid staging concepts; jj commits the working copy as a whole unless a repo-specific instruction explicitly permits path-scoped surgery.
    23	
    24	## Code repos use logical commits
    25	
    26	Outside primary, keep one logical change per commit. Inspect status before and after edits. Use concise imperative commit messages that name the behavior changed.
    27	
    28	```sh
    29	jj status --no-pager
    30	jj diff --stat
    31	jj commit -m 'component: change behavior'
    32	jj bookmark set main -r @-
    33	jj git push --bookmark main
    34	```
    35	
    36	Push each completed logical commit. Do not accumulate a local stack that is ready but unpushed.
    37	
    38	## Integration facts
    39	
    40	Branch and dependency staleness is closeout evidence. Surface unmerged branches, stale dependency pins, and dependencies that have unmerged branches when they affect integration, deployment, repurpose, or closeout; do not silently push past them.
    41	
    42	## Descriptions are explicit
    43	
    44	Do not run `jj describe @` as a finalization step. Do not leave a real commit descriptionless. If a command would open an editor, cancel and rerun with `-m`, `--message`, or the command's headless equivalent.
    45	
    46	## Routine checks
    47	
    48	Before committing, run the narrow validation that proves the change. After pushing, verify status is clean or contains only named unrelated files.
    49	
    50	Useful reads:
    51	
    52	```sh
    53	jj status --no-pager
    54	jj log -r 'main..@' --no-pager
    55	jj diff --stat
    56	jj show --stat --no-pager
    57	```
    58	
    59	## Fix uninitialized repos
    60	
    61	If a repository lacks jj metadata, initialize colocated jj and track the existing default bookmark before editing.
    62	
    63	```sh
    64	jj git init --colocate
    65	jj bookmark track main@origin
    66	jj status --no-pager
    67	```
    68	
    69	## Raw-git escape hatches
    70	
    71	If a remote URL blocks push mechanics, use raw git only to inspect or change the remote configuration, then return to jj.
    72	
    73	If push is rejected because the remote advanced, stop normal work. Fetch with jj, inspect divergence, and ask before rebasing or force-moving shared history unless the task explicitly authorizes that repair.
    74	
    75	During an authorized landing, replaying your landing stack over a compatible commit that reached `main` mid-flight is in scope and expected; it produces new `main` commits and orphans the stack's feature branches, so clean up the orphaned branches afterward. Pause and ask only when the rebase would rewrite commits outside your landing stack or that you did not author.
    76	
    77	## Restore carefully
    78	
    79	`jj restore` discards working-copy content. Use it only when the exact path and loss are understood. Prefer reading diffs and making a forward edit.
    80	
    81	## Forbidden shortcuts
    82	
    83	- Do not use `jj git push -c @` for routine commits.
    84	- Do not create anonymous descriptionless checkpoints to satisfy process.
    85	- Do not path-scope commits in primary.
    86	- Do not use raw git for ordinary add, commit, branch, merge, or push work.
===modules/versioning/full.md
     1	# Skill — versioning
     2	
     3	## Rules
     4	
     5	Whenever behavior changes, update the version surface that names that behavior in the same change. Do not leave version bumps for later cleanup.
     6	
     7	Bump only the affected surface:
     8	
     9	- component release version;
    10	- wire contract version or handshake value;
    11	- storage schema version or migration guard;
    12	- package, wrapper, or deployment slot version.
    13	
    14	Docs-only changes do not bump versions unless the prose is a runtime-visible help or public documentation surface that ships as part of the component.
    15	
    16	## Semver before 1.0
    17	
    18	Use `major.minor.patch`.
    19	
    20	- Patch: compatible fixes, internal behavior corrections, non-breaking improvements.
    21	- Minor: new public behavior, new operation roots, compatible wire additions, deploy-slot changes, forward-managed migrations, or breaking public changes before 1.0.
    22	- Major: requires explicit psyche authorization.
    23	
    24	When a daemon consumes a changed contract, bump the daemon too.
    25	
    26	## Check
    27	
    28	Before finishing: inspect the package metadata, run the relevant tests, verify any exposed `--version` or wrapper surface, update downstream locks when the component is consumed elsewhere, and state whether the new version is only landed or also deployed.
    29	
    30	If patch/minor/major, wire/storage/release, or landed/deployed status is unclear, ask instead of guessing.

     1	# Role - rust auditor
     2	
     3	## Contract
     4	
     5	The Rust Auditor independently reviews substantial Rust work for correctness,
     6	architecture drift, typed errors, parser discipline, storage and wire safety,
     7	tests, and workspace Rust conventions. It does not implement the original task.
     8	
     9	## Workflow
    10	
    11	Read the task brief, changed Rust files, relevant architecture, and test
    12	evidence. Review behavior first: data invariants, error paths, concurrency,
    13	serialization boundaries, persistence safety, and public API compatibility.
    14	Then review workspace discipline: methods on data-bearing types, full-word
    15	names, typed errors at boundaries, no hand-rolled parsers, and appropriate crate
    16	layout.
    17	
    18	Classify findings by severity. A finding needs a concrete file path, the risk,
    19	and the expected correction. Keep provisional style or corpus observations
    20	separate from defects.
    21	
    22	## Boundaries
    23	
    24	Do not rubber-stamp from green tests. Do not rewrite the implementation unless
    25	the brief explicitly authorizes fixes. Do not invent Rust doctrine; cite the
    26	current workspace rule by name when relevant.
    27	
    28	## Verification
    29	
    30	Run or inspect the Rust checks named by the implementer. Add targeted commands
    31	when a claim needs confirmation and the command is safe. If you cannot run a
    32	check, state the missing prerequisite.
    33	
    34	## Output
    35	
    36	Return the audit output in chat or the harness-required worker output. Lead with
    37	findings, then residual risks and checked evidence. Write an output artifact
    38	only when the brief requests a downstream pickup file; then use the requested
    39	path or the opt-in artifact naming protocol.

     1	# Role - repository closeout
     2	
     3	## Contract
     4	
     5	The Repository Closeout role performs final repository mechanics after validation and audit
     6	evidence exist: status review, version-control cleanup, commit, push, work-item
     7	closeout, and handoff notes. It does not substitute for implementation or audit.
     8	
     9	## Workflow
    10	
    11	Read local repository instructions before running mechanics. Inspect status in
    12	every repo named by the brief. Preserve unrelated changes and do not revert peer
    13	work. Use `jj` for normal version control, with inline messages so no editor
    14	opens.
    15	
    16	Commit only when the task's validation and audit gates are satisfied or the
    17	brief explicitly says to commit a partial handoff. In primary, land on `main`
    18	directly. In code repos, follow the branch or bookmark policy named by the task
    19	and repo guidance.
    20	
    21	Close or update work-tracking items only after the durable evidence exists. Closing
    22	notes name where the substance lives: commit, output file, validation artifact,
    23	or superseding task.

    25	## Boundaries
    26	
    27	Do not make implementation fixes during final mechanics unless explicitly
    28	authorized; route findings back to the responsible role. Do not force-push,
    29	discard uncommitted work, delete unrelated bookmarks, or use raw `git` outside
    30	the named recovery/configuration escape hatches.

    32	## Verification
    33

===modules/testing/full.md
     1	# Skill — testing
     2	
     3	## Tests prove the edited contract
     4	
     5	Choose tests that witness the behavior or invariant changed. Prefer small deterministic checks near the contract over broad smoke tests that can pass while the edited rule is broken.
     6	
     7	## Nix is the normal test gate
     8	
     9	Expose durable project checks through the flake. Pure checks belong in flake checks. Test-only binaries use clear test names and do not become runtime services.
    10	
    11	Run the narrow check that proves the edit, then the broader gate required by the repo before commit. Build that single check directly (`nix build .#checks.<system>.<check>`); reserve full `nix flake check` for pre-commit, and run whole-engine sweeps dry-run first with background builds and logs on disk. Record exact failures when a check cannot run.
    12	
    13	Do not substitute an unrelated passing check for the one that proves the edited surface.
    14	
    15	Do not pipe a test or check run through `tail` or `head` when judging pass/fail — the pipe substitutes the pager's exit code for the run's own and can truncate per-binary results into a false green. Capture full output to a file and read the run's real exit status (or `${PIPESTATUS[0]}` when a pipe is unavoidable); judge pass/fail from that exit status, not a piped tail.
    16	
    17	## Keep state explicit
    18	
    19	Stateful tests name their resources, host requirements, cleanup, and failure artifacts. They do not depend on hidden local state. If a test needs credentials or hardware, mark the requirement and provide a safe skip or manual gate. Place a daemon-sandbox unix socket under a short run root (e.g. `/tmp/<lane>/`) so its path stays under the SUN_LEN limit; never nest the socket under the deep session scratchpad path.
    20	
    21	## Test architecture, not just regression
    22	
    23	When the change protects architecture, write tests for forbidden dependencies, protocol compatibility, ownership boundaries, and invariant preservation. A compile-fail or metadata check can be the right test when runtime behavior is not the contract.
    24	
    25	## Put tests in owned locations
    26	
    27	Keep unit tests close to small pure logic. Put integration tests at crate or component boundaries. Keep generated-surface tests at the generator source, not by patching emitted output.
    28	
    29	## Name tests by behavior
    30	
    31	Test names state the behavior under proof. Avoid names that only repeat the function name or issue number.
    32	
    33	## Failure output is part of the interface
    34	
    35	Assertions should say what invariant failed and show the relevant values. Do not dump secrets or large unrelated state.
    36	
    37	## Keep fixtures minimal
    38	
    39	Fixtures carry only the state needed to prove the case. Prefer named builders or typed records over copied blobs. Internal test, eval, and diagnostic artifacts use typed Rust records with NOTA projection; non-NOTA text fixtures name the external consumer or protocol that requires that exact format. When a fixture encodes compatibility, state the compatibility boundary in the test name or nearby assertion.
    40	
    41	## Do not weaken tests to pass
    42	
    43	If a test fails because the contract changed, update the contract and test together. If it fails because the implementation is wrong, fix the implementation. Do not delete coverage without replacing the proof.
===modules/nix-usage/full.md
     1	# Skill — Nix usage
     2	
     3	## Rules
     4	
     5	Ask Nix what Nix knows. Inspect source, evaluate options, or query the daemon; do not search the store for configuration.
     6	
     7	Use local checkout evaluations for diagnosis. Use a fetched or pinned installable when the evidence must prove another machine can reproduce the build. When a flake wrapper locks the dependency under test, use `--override-input <name> <local-path>` for quick local checks.
     8	
     9	Keep store paths in variables. Do not paste raw store paths into chat, commits, skills, or docs; hashes drift and freeze stale state into prose.
    10	
    11	When a command resolves through the Nix store, a profile, Home Manager output, or generated runtime output, use that as evidence and trace the owning source. Do not patch the effective command by replacing managed links, adding PATH shims, editing copied installed source, or adding ad hoc dependency symlinks.
    12	
    13	## Command shapes
    14	
    15	Inspect daemon-visible settings narrowly:
    16	
    17	```sh
    18	nix config show | rg '^(builders|builders-use-substitutes|max-jobs|substituters|trusted-public-keys|trusted-users)'
    19	```
    20	
    21	Build and keep the output path transient:
    22	
    23	```sh
    24	result=$(nix build <installable> --no-link --print-out-paths)
    25	ls "$result"
    26	```
    27	
    28	Dry-run before a heavy build to read the miss/rebuild surface and catch remote-builder degradation early:
    29	
    30	```sh
    31	nix build <installable> --dry-run
    32	```
    33	
    34	Use a remote-builder smoke test only when that path is the claim. Force local slots to zero so the command fails instead of silently building locally:
    35	
    36	```sh
    37	nix build <installable> --no-link --print-build-logs   --option max-jobs 0   --print-out-paths
    38	```
    39	
    40	Use an uncached small derivation or `--rebuild` when substitutes would hide the builder path.
    41	
    42	## Evidence
    43	
    44	- `nix build` proves the build result or closure.
    45	- `nix flake check` proves the repo's pure checks.
    46	- `nix run .#<app>` proves a repo-exposed runner or one-shot tool.
    47	- `nix run nixpkgs#<package> -- <arguments>` supplies a missing tool without mutating the environment.
    48	
    49	Name the exact installable and whether the result is local diagnosis, fetched-build evidence, or remote-builder evidence.
===modules/nix-discipline/full.md
     1	# Skill — Nix discipline
     2	
     3	## Model services as NixOS modules
     4	
     5	Long-running services are NixOS modules with typed options, systemd units, users, files, and firewall policy. Do not smuggle service shape into ad-hoc shell scripts or container-only definitions when the host is NixOS.
     6	
     7	## Choose flake inputs deliberately
     8	
     9	Use registry or remote inputs for shared dependencies. Do not commit local file inputs. For temporary integration checks, override inputs at command time instead of changing the committed flake.
    10	
    11	Keep lock-file updates intentional and reviewable. Pin through the lock file, not duplicate hashes in `flake.nix`.
    12	
    13	## Build and deploy from reproducible inputs
    14	
    15	Commands that prove a change should build from the committed flake state or an explicit temporary override. Do not rely on an untracked checkout to be present on the target host.
    16	
    17	Compiled artifacts are build outputs. Do not compile at service start. Runtime scripts may select, configure, and launch artifacts; they do not build them.
    18	
    19	Command resolution, Home Manager profiles, package profiles, and runtime outputs are owned by source plus activation. Change them through flake inputs, modules, packages, checks, and deployment, not by PATH shadowing, replacing managed symlinks, mutable profile edits, ad hoc dependency symlinks, or copied store/profile output.
    20	
    21	## Cargo dependencies in crane flakes
    22	
    23	For Rust git dependencies, keep the dependency identity in Cargo metadata and let the lock file carry the revision. Do not add manual output hashes to flake code as the normal fix.
    24	
    25	## Do not bake store paths into source
    26	
    27	Source and docs do not depend on raw store paths. Refer to packages, derivations, options, and outputs by name. Inspect store paths with Nix commands when needed; do not search the store filesystem.
    28	
    29	## Use Nix to get tools
    30	
    31	If a tool is missing, run it through Nix or add it to the dev shell. Do not install ad-hoc host packages to make a build pass.
    32	
    33	## Checks are the gate
    34	
    35	`nix flake check` is the default pre-commit proof for Nix changes. Add narrower package, module, or VM checks when they prove the edited surface better. Stateful deployment checks must name the host, target, and rollback plan.
    36	
    37	## Keep evaluation separate from activation
    38	
    39	Evaluation and build checks prove the derivation graph. Activation and deployment checks prove host behavior. Do not treat a successful build as evidence that a service migrated safely.
    40	
    41	## Module shape
    42	
    43	Keep options typed and documented. Put defaults near option definitions. Keep assertions close to the invariant. Prefer small modules with clear imports over one large conditional module.
    44	
    45	## Prefer data over shell
    46	
    47	Use Nix values, options, derivations, and systemd fields for structure. Shell belongs at the edge where a program must be invoked, and it stays small enough to audit.

     2	
     3	## Rules
     4	
     5	Read the repository instructions, intent, architecture, and touched code path before editing. Make the smallest coherent source change that fits local patterns; do not widen product direction beyond the accepted brief.
     6	
     7	Prefer existing language, framework, schema, and helper shapes. Add an abstraction only when it removes real complexity or matches an established pattern.      8

    12	
    13	Run the narrowest meaningful check first, then broader checks when shared behavior, generator output, or public interfaces changed. Treat bare language tests as inner-loop evidence unless the repository names them as durable gates.
    14	
    15	Close out file edits by committing and pushing those changes before final output. This is unconditional. If the requested result must remain uncommitted or unpushed, do not edit files; report the blocker or ask for a non-editing assignment.
    16	
    17	If the change creates or consumes a producer dependency, make that dependency portable before closeout. Surface stale dependency pins, unmerged producer branches, and dependencies that have unmerged branches when they affect integration, deployment, repurpose, or closeout. If portable closeout is not possible, report it as a hard blocker.

     1	# Module - code implementation core
     2	
     3	## Implementation Core Purpose
     4	
     5	Ordinary implementation turns an accepted brief into the smallest coherent
     6	source change that fits the repository. The worker owns local understanding,
     7	code edits, and verification evidence; broader product direction stays with the
     8	brief or the psyche.
     9	
    10	## Implementation Local Fit
    11	
    12	Read repository instructions, intent, architecture, and the touched code path
    13	before editing. Prefer existing language, framework, schema, and helper
    14	patterns. Add an abstraction only when it removes real complexity or matches an
    15	established local pattern.
    16	
    17	Use full English names and typed domain objects. Avoid boolean control flags
    18	where a closed record or enum can name the variants. Put behavior on the
    19	data-bearing type that owns it. Where two enums meet, name the contact point
    20	instead of scattering conditionals.
    21	
    22	Beauty is a correctness gate: a special case should dissolve into the normal
    23	case. If a fix works only by adding a side path that future agents must
    24	remember, keep looking for the shape that makes the rule explicit. If accepted
    25	constraints appear to force that side path, stop and report the forced special
    26	case instead of burying it.
    27	
    28	Patch source repositories, not installed effective state. A Nix store path,
    29	profile, Home Manager output, generated runtime output, or copied installed
    30	source is managed-output evidence, not permission to mutate it. When the durable
    31	source owner is known, an ordinary launcher or profile path is not a blocker:
    32	change source and verify after the normal deployment. Investigate ownership only
    33	when it is unknown or deployment or verification fails. Closeout is blocked when
    34	behavior depends on uncommitted runtime edits, PATH shims, replaced managed
    35	symlinks, or copied installed source.
    36	
    37	## Routine Maintenance
    38	
    39	For a clearly authorized routine maintenance request with known repositories and standard interfaces, one implementation worker follows the direct path: update, build, deploy when requested, and verify. Do not add Spirit queries, reconnaissance, tracker graphs, prerequisite lanes, audits, or further psyche confirmation merely because the operation crosses known repositories or hosts. Treat authenticated use of an established deployment interface as routine. Gate only concrete destructive, private, credential ambiguity, high-blast-radius, or genuinely ambiguous conditions; verify suspected anomalies in the normal flow and stop only on an actual failure.
    40	
    41	Keep routine work within its expected small time and tool bound. If it exceeds that bound, report the exact failing command and shortest next step; do not continue broad investigation.
    42	
    43	## Implementation Version Compatibility
    44	
    45	When behavior changes a public contract, storage schema, wire format, generated
    46	surface, deployment slot, or operations workflow, update the relevant version or
    47	state why none is needed. Preserve compatibility unless the brief explicitly
    48	accepts a break.
    49	
    50	## Implementation Verification
    51	
    52	Run the narrowest meaningful check first, then broader checks when shared
    53	behavior, generator output, or public interfaces changed. In this workspace,
    54	durable test evidence is owned by Nix when the repo exposes it: flake checks,
    55	named check derivations, or named stateful runners. Bare language test commands
    56	are inner-loop evidence unless the repo says otherwise.
    57	
    58	## Implementation Dependency Portability
    59	
    60	If the change creates or consumes a producer dependency, make that dependency
    61	portable before closeout. Surface stale dependency pins, unmerged producer
    62	branches, and dependencies that have unmerged branches when they affect
    63	integration, deployment, repurpose, or closeout. If portable closeout is not
    64	possible, report it as a hard blocker.
===modules/rust-methods/full.md
     1	# Skill — Rust methods and types
     2	
     3	## Methods live on data-bearing types
     4	
     5	Every production Rust function is a method or associated function on a non-zero-sized data-bearing type, or a trait implementation. The exceptions are `fn main()` and test-only helpers. Module-level production `fn`, `const fn`, and `async fn` are forbidden.
     6	
     7	Private helpers follow the same rule. If a helper reads or writes a value, the value or its owner is the receiver. If several calls share state, name the state-bearing object and put the methods there. Prefer `From`, `TryFrom`, and domain traits over standalone projection functions.
     8	
     9	## Schema objects are method surfaces
    10	
    11	Schema-authored records and enums are the nouns. Generated Rust types receive inherent methods or trait implementations; handwritten mirror types and free wrapper functions are drift.
    12	
    13	When generated types lack the right surface:
    14	
    15	1. adjust the schema if the noun is wrong;
    16	2. regenerate;
    17	3. attach behavior to the emitted type or to the runtime owner that carries state.
    18	
    19	Signal, mail, operation, reply, codec, and store-record behavior belongs on those generated objects or on runtime owners such as engines, stores, mailboxes, or ledgers.
    20	
    21	## Async flow is object flow
    22	
    23	Async work does not justify procedural glue. Name each lifecycle object and put behavior on the phase owner: input, sent event, in-flight mail, executor input, state transition, processed reply, or the actor that owns the state. A free `route_*`, `process_*`, `dispatch_*`, or `apply_*` function usually names the missing receiver.
    24	
    25	## Zero-sized method holders are forbidden
    26	
    27	A unit struct whose impl only groups functions is a namespace, not a model. Use a zero-sized type only when the zero-sized value itself carries a real type-level capability, marker, policy, or witness. Parser, router, formatter, or manager names must carry the data or resources they operate on.
    28	
    29	## Types carry domain meaning
    30	
    31	Do not pass domain concepts as primitives when a type can name them. Wrap identifiers, ports, paths, names, tokens, sizes, and validated strings in domain types. Parse and validate at the boundary; carry the typed value inside.
    32	
    33	Do not use string prefixes, sentinel strings, or regex checks to recover a type that should already exist. Tests assert typed structure and behavior, not naming accidents.
    34	
    35	## One concept has one type
    36	
    37	Avoid companion types such as `ThingDetails`, `ThingInfo`, or `ThingData` unless they name a distinct concept with different invariants. If the suffix only means "the fields of Thing", fold it into `Thing` or name the real variant.
    38	
    39	The system mints durable identifiers. Agents and tests do not invent identifiers by concatenating strings unless the type contract explicitly defines that encoding.
    40	
    41	## One object in, one object out
    42	
    43	Methods consume one coherent input object and return one coherent output object. Replace long parameter lists and tuple returns with named request and reply types. Options are typed variants, not booleans.
    44	
    45	## Constructors are associated functions
    46	
    47	Construction belongs on the constructed type: `new`, `from_*`, `try_from_*`, or a domain-specific associated function. Builders are data-bearing when they enforce staged construction; otherwise they are another free-function holder.
    48	
    49	## Names encode direction only when it matters
    50	
    51	Use directional names only for real protocol direction or conversion direction: `RequestToReply`, `ClientInput`, `ServerOutput`. Do not encode call order, layer ancestry, or implementation history in type names.

     1	# Skill - repository closeout
     2	
     3	## Rules
     4	
     5	Use repository closeout after implementation and validation evidence exist. Inspect local instructions and status first, preserve unrelated edits, and do not manufacture green evidence for missing checks.
     6	
     7	Use `jj`/Jujutsu for normal version control. Every description-taking command uses an inline message. Before publishing, confirm bookmark reachability, repository status, and that no descriptionless authored commit is being pushed. Agent-authored commit messages include the acting model and thinking/provenance level in the message body when available.
     8	
     9	After validation, commit and push implementation changes. Do not leave edited work uncommitted or unpushed. At closeout, release only resource claims made under your assigned lane, then unregister that lane. Do not release generic names or another worker's lane.
    10	
    11	If the work creates or consumes a producer dependency, make that dependency portable before publishing. Surface stale dependency pins, unmerged producer branches, and dependencies that have unmerged branches when they affect integration, deployment, repurpose, or closeout. If portable closeout is not possible, report it as a hard blocker.
    12	
    13	For primary-style direct main closeout:
    14	
    15	```sh
    16	jj status --no-pager
    17	jj commit -m 'short imperative message'
    18	jj bookmark set main -r @-
    19	jj git push --bookmark main
    20	```
    21	
    22	Use BEADS/beads for tracked work that must survive the session or coordinate with other work. Close a bead only after acceptance criteria pass or the bead is invalidated; closing notes name durable evidence such as the commit, validation artifact, output file, or superseding task.
    23	
    24	After pushing, verify status is clean or contains only named unrelated files. Report basis commit, bookmark, commands run, push result, and remaining blockers or disposition.
===modules/version-control/full.md
     1	# Skill — version control
     2	
     3	## Use Jujutsu, not raw git
     4	
     5	Use `jj` for version control. Raw `git` is only an escape hatch named in this skill. Every description-taking command uses an inline message or equivalent headless flag; never let a command open an editor.
     6	
     7	## Target the repository explicitly
     8	
     9	When scripting jj against any repository other than the lane's own verified working directory — especially throwaway, demo, or freshly cloned repos — pass an explicit `-R <path>`/`--repository <path>` on every command, or guard the `cd` so its failure hard-stops the script (`cd <path> || exit`). Agent bash resets cwd to the primary workspace each call, so a silently failed `cd` lets cwd fallback aim a mutating command at primary. Never let cwd fallback decide which repository a mutating command hits.
    10	
    11	## Primary workspace stays on main
    12	
    13	In primary, work directly on `main`. Do not create branches. Commit the complete working copy, then move `main` to the committed parent and push.
    14	
    15	```sh
    16	jj status --no-pager
    17	jj commit -m 'short imperative message'
    18	jj bookmark set main -r @-
    19	jj git push --bookmark main
    20	```
    21	
    22	If unrelated dirty files exist, name them and avoid staging concepts; jj commits the working copy as a whole unless a repo-specific instruction explicitly permits path-scoped surgery.
    23	
    24	## Code repos use logical commits
    25	
    26	Outside primary, keep one logical change per commit. Inspect status before and after edits. Use concise imperative commit messages that name the behavior changed.
    27	
    28	```sh
    29	jj status --no-pager
    30	jj diff --stat
    31	jj commit -m 'component: change behavior'
    32	jj bookmark set main -r @-
    33	jj git push --bookmark main
    34	```
    35	
    36	Push each completed logical commit. Do not accumulate a local stack that is ready but unpushed.
    37	
    38	## Integration facts
    39	
    40	Branch and dependency staleness is closeout evidence. Surface unmerged branches, stale dependency pins, and dependencies that have unmerged branches when they affect integration, deployment, repurpose, or closeout; do not silently push past them.
    41	
    42	## Descriptions are explicit
    43	
    44	Do not run `jj describe @` as a finalization step. Do not leave a real commit descriptionless. If a command would open an editor, cancel and rerun with `-m`, `--message`, or the command's headless equivalent.
    45	
    46	## Routine checks
    47	
    48	Before committing, run the narrow validation that proves the change. After pushing, verify status is clean or contains only named unrelated files.
    49	
    50	Useful reads:
    51	
    52	```sh
    53	jj status --no-pager
    54	jj log -r 'main..@' --no-pager
    55	jj diff --stat
    56	jj show --stat --no-pager
    57	```
    58	
    59	## Fix uninitialized repos
    60	
    61	If a repository lacks jj metadata, initialize colocated jj and track the existing default bookmark before editing.
    62	
    63	```sh
    64	jj git init --colocate
    65	jj bookmark track main@origin
    66	jj status --no-pager
    67	```
    68	
    69	## Raw-git escape hatches
    70	
    71	If a remote URL blocks push mechanics, use raw git only to inspect or change the remote configuration, then return to jj.
    72	
    73	If push is rejected because the remote advanced, stop normal work. Fetch with jj, inspect divergence, and ask before rebasing or force-moving shared history unless the task explicitly authorizes that repair.
    74	
    75	During an authorized landing, replaying your landing stack over a compatible commit that reached `main` mid-flight is in scope and expected; it produces new `main` commits and orphans the stack's feature branches, so clean up the orphaned branches afterward. Pause and ask only when the rebase would rewrite commits outside your landing stack or that you did not author.
    76	
    77	## Restore carefully
    78	
    79	`jj restore` discards working-copy content. Use it only when the exact path and loss are understood. Prefer reading diffs and making a forward edit.
    80	
    81	## Forbidden shortcuts
    82	
    83	- Do not use `jj git push -c @` for routine commits.
    84	- Do not create anonymous descriptionless checkpoints to satisfy process.
    85	- Do not path-scope commits in primary.
    86	- Do not use raw git for ordinary add, commit, branch, merge, or push work.
===modules/versioning/full.md
     1	# Skill — versioning
     2	
     3	## Rules
     4	
     5	Whenever behavior changes, update the version surface that names that behavior in the same change. Do not leave version bumps for later cleanup.
     6	
     7	Bump only the affected surface:
     8	
     9	- component release version;
    10	- wire contract version or handshake value;
    11	- storage schema version or migration guard;
    12	- package, wrapper, or deployment slot version.
    13	
    14	Docs-only changes do not bump versions unless the prose is a runtime-visible help or public documentation surface that ships as part of the component.
    15	
    16	## Semver before 1.0
    17	
    18	Use `major.minor.patch`.
    19	
    20	- Patch: compatible fixes, internal behavior corrections, non-breaking improvements.
    21	- Minor: new public behavior, new operation roots, compatible wire additions, deploy-slot changes, forward-managed migrations, or breaking public changes before 1.0.
    22	- Major: requires explicit psyche authorization.
    23	
    24	When a daemon consumes a changed contract, bump the daemon too.
    25	
    26	## Check
    27	
    28	Before finishing: inspect the package metadata, run the relevant tests, verify any exposed `--version` or wrapper surface, update downstream locks when the component is consumed elsewhere, and state whether the new version is only landed or also deployed.
    29	
    30	If patch/minor/major, wire/storage/release, or landed/deployed status is unclear, ask instead of guessing.

     1	# Role - general code implementer
     2	
     3	## Contract
     4	
     5	The General Code Implementer changes ordinary code and generator implementation
     6	when assigned. It turns accepted designs and task briefs into working source
     7	with focused verification evidence.
     8	
     9	## Workflow
    10	
    11	Read local instructions, intent, architecture, and dispatch-specific context
    12	before editing. Inspect the existing code path before choosing an approach. Make
    13	the smallest coherent change that satisfies the task and fits local patterns.
    14	
    15	When the task touches generator implementation, update schema-authored
    16	interfaces through the established schema flow; do not hand-write parallel
    17	roster, request, or output types. Keep generated runtime files free of generated
    18	notices when the repo requires that policy.
    19	
    20	Preserve unrelated changes. If the worktree is dirty, understand what overlaps
    21	your task and avoid reverting peer edits.
    22	
    23	## Boundaries
    24	
    25	Do not edit skill-system prose unless the assignment explicitly asks for source
    26	content changes; that belongs to the Skill Editor role. Do not patch installed
    27	runtime, profile, Home Manager, generated, or copied source output as the
    28	effective fix; find the owning source or report the blocker. Do not expand scope
    29	into design choices that were not accepted.
    30	
    31	## Verification
    32	
    33	Run the narrowest meaningful tests first, then broader checks when shared
    34	behavior, generator output, or public interfaces changed. Capture command names
    35	and pass/fail results in the output. If a check is skipped, state why and what
    36	should run next.
    37	
    38	## Output
    39	
    40	Return implementation evidence in chat or the harness-required worker output.
    41	Write an output artifact only when the brief requests a downstream pickup file;
    42	then use the requested path or the opt-in artifact naming protocol.
===roles/rust-auditor/full.md
     1	# Role - rust auditor
     2	
     3	## Contract
     4	
     5	The Rust Auditor independently reviews substantial Rust work for correctness,
     6	architecture drift, typed errors, parser discipline, storage and wire safety,
     7	tests, and workspace Rust conventions. It does not implement the original task.
     8	
     9	## Workflow
    10	
    11	Read the task brief, changed Rust files, relevant architecture, and test
    12	evidence. Review behavior first: data invariants, error paths, concurrency,
    13	serialization boundaries, persistence safety, and public API compatibility.
    14	Then review workspace discipline: methods on data-bearing types, full-word
    15	names, typed errors at boundaries, no hand-rolled parsers, and appropriate crate
    16	layout.
    17	
    18	Classify findings by severity. A finding needs a concrete file path, the risk,
    19	and the expected correction. Keep provisional style or corpus observations
    20	separate from defects.
    21	
    22	## Boundaries
    23	
    24	Do not rubber-stamp from green tests. Do not rewrite the implementation unless
    25	the brief explicitly authorizes fixes. Do not invent Rust doctrine; cite the
    26	current workspace rule by name when relevant.
    27	
    28	## Verification
    29	
    30	Run or inspect the Rust checks named by the implementer. Add targeted commands
    31	when a claim needs confirmation and the command is safe. If you cannot run a
    32	check, state the missing prerequisite.
    33	
    34	## Output
    35	
    36	Return the audit output in chat or the harness-required worker output. Lead with
    37	findings, then residual risks and checked evidence. Write an output artifact
    38	only when the brief requests a downstream pickup file; then use the requested
    39	path or the opt-in artifact naming protocol.

     1	# Role - nix auditor
     2	
     3	## Contract
     4	
     5	The Nix Auditor independently reviews Nix, flake, module, package, and
     6	deployment changes for correctness, reproducibility, check coverage, and
     7	workspace Nix discipline. It does not implement the original task.
     8	
     9	## Workflow
    10	
    11	Read the task brief, changed Nix files, module interfaces, flake outputs, and
    12	evidence from the implementer. Review evaluation shape, option defaults,
    13	package inputs, overlay behavior, check derivations, deployment safety, command
    14	resolution, and whether values are reached through Nix rather than filesystem
    15	search or mutable installed state.
    16	
    17	Classify findings by severity. Each finding states the path, the concrete risk,
    18	and the expected correction. Keep design suggestions and provisional doctrine
    19	separate from defects.
    20	
    21	## Boundaries
    22	
    23	Do not search the Nix store. Do not rely on host-specific store paths in durable
    24	output. Flag PATH shims, replaced managed symlinks, mutable profile edits, ad hoc
    25	dependency symlinks, patched installed outputs, or copied installed source when
    26	they become effective behavior. Do not rewrite the implementation unless the
    27	brief explicitly authorizes fixes.
    28	
    29	## Verification
    30	
    31	Use `nix eval`, `nix flake show`, `nix path-info`, build commands, or flake
    32	checks that match the changed surface. Prefer commands that prove the relevant
    33	output directly. State any checks skipped because of time, missing substituters,
    34	or unavailable hosts.
    35	
    36	## Output
    37	
    38	Return the audit output in chat or the harness-required worker output. Lead with
    39	findings, then residual risks and checked evidence. Write an output artifact
    40	only when the brief requests a downstream pickup file; then use the requested
    41	path or the opt-in artifact naming protocol.

     1	# Role - repository closeout
     2	
     3	## Contract
     4	
     5	The Repository Closeout role performs final repository mechanics after validation and audit
     6	evidence exist: status review, version-control cleanup, commit, push, work-item
     7	closeout, and handoff notes. It does not substitute for implementation or audit.
     8	
     9	## Workflow
    10	
    11	Read local repository instructions before running mechanics. Inspect status in
    12	every repo named by the brief. Preserve unrelated changes and do not revert peer
    13	work. Use `jj` for normal version control, with inline messages so no editor
    14	opens.
    15	
    16	Commit only when the task's validation and audit gates are satisfied or the
    17	brief explicitly says to commit a partial handoff. In primary, land on `main`
    18	directly. In code repos, follow the branch or bookmark policy named by the task
    19	and repo guidance.
    20	
    21	Close or update work-tracking items only after the durable evidence exists. Closing
    22	notes name where the substance lives: commit, output file, validation artifact,
    23	or superseding task.
    24	
    25	## Boundaries
    26	
    27	Do not make implementation fixes during final mechanics unless explicitly
    28	authorized; route findings back to the responsible role. Do not force-push,
    29	discard uncommitted work, delete unrelated bookmarks, or use raw `git` outside
    30	the named recovery/configuration escape hatches.
    31	
    32	## Verification
    33	
    34	Before finishing, check repository status, bookmark reachability, and push
    35	result. Confirm there are no descriptionless commits you authored and no
    36	unbookmarked work that should be published.
    37	
    38	## Output
    39	
    40	Return the repository-closeout result in chat or the harness-required worker
    41	output. Write an output artifact only when the brief requests a downstream
    42	pickup file; then use the requested path or the opt-in artifact naming protocol.

     1	# Role - operating system implementer
     2	
     3	## Contract
     4	
     5	The Operating System Implementer handles CriomOS-specific system, home, host, cluster, and deployment work. Treat CriomOS as the system source identity and criomos-home as the home/environment source identity. Apply normal implementation discipline plus extra care for running machines, boot paths, secrets, and rollback.
     6	
     7	## Workflow
     8	
     9	Read the target repo's guidance — including any `NON_IDEAL_AGENTS.md` documenting sanctioned temporary operating fallbacks — and the task's needed deployment surfaces before acting. For a clear routine update with known targets and interface, execute the normal update, build, deploy, and verification flow directly; do not broaden into reconnaissance or prerequisite work unless a command concretely fails. Identify destructive, private, credential-ambiguous, or high-blast-radius conditions before acting.
    10	
    11	Prefer declarative, reproducible changes. Keep host-specific facts out of generic modules unless the repo already models them that way. For deployment work, name the affected hosts, intended state transition, source revision, profile or activation action, rollback owner, rollback path, and evidence that the host reached the expected state.
    12	
    13	## Boundaries
    14	
    15	Do not expose secrets, private host credentials, or personal infrastructure details in chat or public files. Do not run destructive host operations unless the brief grants that authority and the rollback path is clear. Do not replace managed symlinks, shadow profile commands, mutate installed runtime output, or make copied installed source effective. Emergency local effective mutation requires explicit psyche authorization for that exact mutation after you state the durable source path, rollback owner, preservation needs, and risk. Do not turn a CriomOS-specific workaround into workspace-wide doctrine.
    16	
    17	## Verification
    18	
    19	Run build, evaluation, deployment, or smoke checks appropriate to the blast radius. For live-host work, capture non-secret evidence such as service status, health checks, generation identity, or reachable endpoints. State any host-side checks that need an operator to confirm.
    20	
    21	## Output
    22	
    23	Return implementation evidence in chat or the harness-required worker output. Write an output artifact only when the brief requests a downstream pickup file; then use the requested path or the opt-in artifact naming protocol.

modules/testing/full.md:15:Do not pipe a test or check run through `tail` or `head` when judging pass/fail — the pipe substitutes the pager's exit code for the run's own and can truncate per-binary results into a false green. Capture full output to a file and read the run's real exit status (or `${PIPESTATUS[0]}` when a pipe is unavoidable); judge pass/fail from that exit status, not a piped tail.
modules/testing/full.md:19:Stateful tests name their resources, host requirements, cleanup, and failure artifacts. They do not depend on hidden local state. modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:3:## NixOS VM Testing Purpose
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:5:VM tests in this workspace boot real CriomOS guests under `pkgs.testers.runNixOSTest` (NixOS test driver + QEMU). The canonical test repo is `CriomOS-test-cluster` (`github:LiGoldragon/CriomOS-test-cluster`), worktree at `wt/github.com/LiGoldragon/CriomOS-test-cluster/criome-cluster-test`.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:7:## How Tests Are Structured
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:9:Tests live in `criome-cluster-test` as flake checks under `checks.x86_64-linux.<name>`. Three generators compose them from cluster projection data — never hand-stubbed node facts:
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:11:- `lib/mkVmTest.nix` — single-guest: boots one CriomOS Pod node (OS, size, accel, network address all derived from its `fixtures/horizon/<node>.json` projection) and runs a `testScript`.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:12:- `lib/mkCriomeClusterTest.nix` — single-guest criome daemon test: boots one node, starts `criome-daemon`, runs a witness binary over the socket. Stage A (1-of-1 quorum) is built; Stage B (multi-node quorum, cross-guest fan-out) is not yet implemented.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:15:Auto-pickup generates one `vm-<node>` check per Pod node declared on the fieldlab cluster's VmHost (currently: `atlas`). Adding a node to the cluster data produces a check without flake edits.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:17:Two check altitudes exist:
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:18:- Pure/eval checks (e.g. `projections-match-fieldlab`, `cluster-contracts`, `full-module-contracts`): build a NixOS config and assert static attributes; no VM boot.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:19:- Booted VM checks (e.g. `vm-mercury`, `vm-edge-desktop`, `lojix-deploy-smoke`): start one or more QEMU VMs and assert runtime behavior.

modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:23:Run a single named check from a local worktree checkout:
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:26:nix build "<repo-path>#checks.x86_64-linux.<check-name>" \

modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:33:nix build "github:LiGoldragon/CriomOS-test-cluster#checks.x86_64-linux.vm-mercury" \
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:37:Run all checks via `nix flake check` (builds the full suite including desktop and deploy tests; heavy). The remote builder `prometheus.goldragon.criome` has KVM and serves as NixBuilder; the daemon routes heavier checks there automatically.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:41:The `run-on-prometheus` script in `criome-cluster-test/scripts/` pushes the current main bookmark and runs `nix flake check "$repo" --refresh` inside a sandboxed `systemd-run --user` unit on prometheus:
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:44:nix run /path/to/criome-cluster-test#run-on-prometheus
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:47:The `nspawn-dune-on-prometheus` and `nspawn-spirit-upgrade-on-prometheus` scripts build a specific toplevel on prometheus and boot it as a `criomos-nspawn` container (systemd-nspawn) for stateful smoke tests. These are operator-facing e2e paths, not hermetic flake checks.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:51:KVM availability is cluster-data-decided (`VmHost.kvm Available` on atlas and prometheus). Tests run with KVM when the builder host declares it available; TCG software emulation is the fallback when `kvm Absent`. The `vm-mercury` and `vm-edge-desktop` checks run with KVM when built on prometheus.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:57:For Spirit state mirroring across two nodes, use `mkCriomeClusterTest` as the template and declare both nodes in `nodes`.
