modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:1:# Module - NixOS VM testing
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:3:## NixOS VM Testing Purpose
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:5:VM tests in this workspace boot real CriomOS guests under `pkgs.testers.runNixOSTest` (NixOS test driver + QEMU). The canonical test repo is `CriomOS-test-cluster` (`github:LiGoldragon/CriomOS-test-cluster`), worktree at `wt/github.com/LiGoldragon/CriomOS-test-cluster/criome-cluster-test`.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:7:## How Tests Are Structured
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:9:Tests live in `criome-cluster-test` as flake checks under `checks.x86_64-linux.<name>`. Three generators compose them from cluster projection data — never hand-stubbed node facts:
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:11:- `lib/mkVmTest.nix` — single-guest: boots one CriomOS Pod node (OS, size, accel, network address all derived from its `fixtures/horizon/<node>.json` projection) and runs a `testScript`.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:12:- `lib/mkCriomeClusterTest.nix` — single-guest criome daemon test: boots one node, starts `criome-daemon`, runs a witness binary over the socket. Stage A (1-of-1 quorum) is built; Stage B (multi-node quorum, cross-guest fan-out) is not yet implemented.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:15:Auto-pickup generates one `vm-<node>` check per Pod node declared on the fieldlab cluster's VmHost (currently: `atlas`). Adding a node to the cluster data produces a check without flake edits.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:17:Two check altitudes exist:
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:18:- Pure/eval checks (e.g. `projections-match-fieldlab`, `cluster-contracts`, `full-module-contracts`): build a NixOS config and assert static attributes; no VM boot.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:19:- Booted VM checks (e.g. `vm-mercury`, `vm-edge-desktop`, `lojix-deploy-smoke`): start one or more QEMU VMs and assert runtime behavior.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:21:## Running VM Tests Locally
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:23:Run a single named check from a local worktree checkout:
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:26:nix build "<repo-path>#checks.x86_64-linux.<check-name>" \
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:30:Example — the lean TestVm boot check (17 s locally, KVM via remote builder):
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:33:nix build "github:LiGoldragon/CriomOS-test-cluster#checks.x86_64-linux.vm-mercury" \
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:37:Run all checks via `nix flake check` (builds the full suite including desktop and deploy tests; heavy). The remote builder `prometheus.goldragon.criome` has KVM and serves as NixBuilder; the daemon routes heavier checks there automatically.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:41:The `run-on-prometheus` script in `criome-cluster-test/scripts/` pushes the current main bookmark and runs `nix flake check "$repo" --refresh` inside a sandboxed `systemd-run --user` unit on prometheus:
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:44:nix run /path/to/criome-cluster-test#run-on-prometheus
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:47:The `nspawn-dune-on-prometheus` and `nspawn-spirit-upgrade-on-prometheus` scripts build a specific toplevel on prometheus and boot it as a `criomos-nspawn` container (systemd-nspawn) for stateful smoke tests. These are operator-facing e2e paths, not hermetic flake checks.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:51:KVM availability is cluster-data-decided (`VmHost.kvm Available` on atlas and prometheus). Tests run with KVM when the builder host declares it available; TCG software emulation is the fallback when `kvm Absent`. The `vm-mercury` and `vm-edge-desktop` checks run with KVM when built on prometheus.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:57:For Spirit state mirroring across two nodes, use `mkCriomeClusterTest` as the template and declare both nodes in `nodes`. Stage B of the criome cluster test (cross-guest quorum fan-out) is not yet implemented; a Spirit-specific two-node mirror test would be new work following the `lojix-deploy-smoke` model.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:61:`runNixOSTest` VM disks are ephemeral per run; no state carries between check invocations. The `criomos-nspawn` path on prometheus creates and destroys containers per script run. Cross-run state persistence is not provided by the existing infrastructure; it requires either persistent volumes on a dedicated host or a stateful service on a real CriomOS node.
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:65:- A NixOS/CriomOS builder with KVM available for fast VM tests (prometheus serves this role).
modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:67:- The Rust toolchain pinned in `channel-rust-stable.toml` must match the current fenix channel; a hash mismatch in that fixed-output derivation blocks checks that build Rust components.
modules/spirit-query/full.md:72:    (Quality [All Testing])
modules/rust-storage-and-wire/full.md:7:Daemons are not text systems. They do not parse or emit JSON or NOTA. Internal tools, tests, evals, fixtures, diagnostics, and new surfaces use typed Rust records with NOTA projection. Use JSON, YAML, CSV, TOML, or other non-NOTA text only when a named external consumer or protocol requires that exact format, such as a provider HTTP API. Keep the adapter, client, CLI, tool, or harness at the edge and convert immediately into typed domain values.
modules/push-not-pull/full.md:9:3. **Write the consumer as a subscriber.** No `sleep(N)` in its main loop, no interval timers, no "check every K seconds."
modules/push-not-pull/full.md:48:- **"Check every interval, debounce flickers"** → the debounce is hiding the poll; use the push source.
modules/push-not-pull/full.md:49:- **Asking an LLM agent to "check inbox every few turns"** → a router pushes the inbox into the harness terminal stream; the model doesn't pull.
modules/structural-forms/full.md:15:When adding or pruning a form, update grammar, AST shape, examples, and round-trip tests together. A syntax rule without a data witness is incomplete.
modules/nix-discipline/full.md:9:Use registry or remote inputs for shared dependencies. Do not commit local file inputs. For temporary integration checks, override inputs at command time instead of changing the committed flake.
modules/nix-discipline/full.md:19:Command resolution, Home Manager profiles, package profiles, and runtime outputs are owned by source plus activation. Change them through flake inputs, modules, packages, checks, and deployment, not by PATH shadowing, replacing managed symlinks, mutable profile edits, ad hoc dependency symlinks, or copied store/profile output.
modules/nix-discipline/full.md:21:## Cargo dependencies in crane flakes
modules/nix-discipline/full.md:23:For Rust git dependencies, keep the dependency identity in Cargo metadata and let the lock file carry the revision. Do not add manual output hashes to flake code as the normal fix.
modules/nix-discipline/full.md:33:## Checks are the gate
modules/nix-discipline/full.md:39:Evaluation and build checks prove the derivation graph. Activation and deployment checks prove host behavior. Do not treat a successful build as evidence that a service migrated safely.
modules/repo-intent/full.md:21:When new psyche intent affects a repo's design, implementation, tests, or operating direction, capture through the accepted intent path and manifest the repo-facing substance into `ARCHITECTURE.md` (or the relevant code stub) before the repo's guidance goes stale.
modules/repo-intent/full.md:23:On substantial repo work, read `ARCHITECTURE.md` on entry, then check whether recent accepted intent affecting that repo is reflected there. Close gaps as part of the work, not as a separate future sweep.
modules/component-architecture/full.md:5:Keep the component split visible in code, manifests, tests, and dependencies.
modules/component-architecture/full.md:40:messages. Use NOTA for authored configuration, seeds, tests, and diagnostics;
modules/component-architecture/full.md:64:Tests prove the boundaries: contract round trips in contract crates, CLI-through-daemon
modules/versioning/full.md:26:## Check
modules/versioning/full.md:28:Before finishing: inspect the package metadata, run the relevant tests, verify any exposed `--version` or wrapper surface, update downstream locks when the component is consumed elsewhere, and state whether the new version is only landed or also deployed.

1	# Module - Nix core
     2	
     3	## Nix Core Purpose
     4	
     5	Nix work in this workspace is reproducible, remote-addressable, and exposed
     6	through named flake surfaces. This module carries the compact rule set for
     7	implementation and audit packets.
     8	
     9	## Nix Source And Inputs
    10	
    11	Use portable flake inputs such as `github:<owner>/<repo>` for sibling
    12	repositories. Pin exact revisions in `flake.lock`, not by hashes in
    13	`flake.nix`. Do not commit `path:` or `git+file://` inputs or overrides; they
    14	make builds depend on one machine's checkout.
    15	
    16	For multi-repo testing, commit and push the participating refs, then use remote
    17	`--override-input` values. Do not test a deployable stack through local
    18	filesystem inputs.
    19	
    20	## Managed Runtime Boundaries
    21	
    22	Treat the effective system as Nix-managed by default. Change command resolution,
    23	Home Manager outputs, profile links, package outputs, and runtime artifacts
    24	through source, flake inputs, lock files, builds or checks, and deployment.
    25	
    26	Do not make mutable installed state the fix: no PATH shadowing, managed-symlink
    27	replacement, mutable profile edits, ad hoc dependency symlinks, patched store or
    28	profile outputs, or copied installed source as the effective runtime. Claims on
    29	source paths do not grant ownership of generated, deployed, profile, or
    30	Nix-managed outputs.
    31	
    32	Read-only inspection, byte-for-byte evidence backups, and isolated repro copies
    33	are allowed when the active role permits them. They must not become effective
    34	runtime, profile, or system behavior. Emergency local effective mutation requires
    35	explicit psyche authorization for that exact mutation after the worker states the
    36	durable source path, rollback owner, preservation needs, and risk.
    37	
    38	Closeout is blocked when behavior depends on uncommitted runtime edits, PATH
    39	shims, replaced managed symlinks, or copied installed source.
    40	
    41	## Nix Modules And Services
    42	
    43	CriomOS services are NixOS modules or typed systemd services, directly on a host
    44	or inside a contained node. Prefer declarative modules, package upstream sources
    45	natively, and make secrets, state, users, ports, and checks visible in the Nix
    46	shape.
    47	
    48	Runtime compilation caches, generated configs, and build artifacts that affect
    49	startup behavior belong in derivations when feasible, so rebuilds are
    50	repeatable and cold starts do not depend on mutable user state.
    51	
    52	## Nix Commands
    53	
    54	Use Nix commands that prove the surface directly: `nix eval`, `nix flake show`,
    55	`nix path-info`, `nix build`, `nix run`, and `nix flake check`. Do not search
    56	the Nix store filesystem. Do not record raw store paths in durable prose as the
    57	proof of correctness.
    58	
    59	## Nix Tests And Safety
    60	
    61	Expose durable checks as flake checks, named apps, packages, or scripts entered
    62	through Nix. Stateful checks name their state directory and leave inspectable
    63	artifacts. VM or live-host checks run only when the host is authorized for that
    64	class of work.
    65	
    66	Keep secrets transient. Do not put secret values in Nix store paths, logs,
    67	reports, chat, commits, or generated outputs.
     1	# Skill — main feature integration
     2	
     3	Use this when updating a code repo's `main` with a feature that is not already
     4	green. This applies to code repos under `/git`, not primary.
     5	
     6	## Flow
     7	
     8	1. Start from the current `main` head: `jj new main`.
     9	2. Work on a named integration bookmark while the feature is not green.
    10	3. Keep each affected repo on a main-based branch; do not stack new work on stale
    11	   peer integration branches unless the task is explicitly to integrate that
    12	   branch.
    13	4. Test the branch family together. Temporary local path overrides are allowed
    14	   during testing, but remove them from the merge-ready state unless the branch
    15	   dependency is intentional and documented.
    16	5. When the candidate is green, fetch/recheck main. If main moved, rebase the
    17	   branch onto the new main and rerun the relevant tests.
    18	6. Land main in dependency order. For a compiler stack, land producers before
    19	   consumers, then update consumer lockfiles against the newly landed main.
    20	7. Push main only after the post-rebase checks are green.
    21	
    22	## Reporting
    23	
    24	State the basis commit, the branch bookmark, the exact temporary overrides used
    25	for testing, the tests run, and whether final main landing happened or remains
    26	blocked on an upstream branch landing.
    35	
    36	Code repositories keep one logical change per commit. Follow the repo's branch
    37	or bookmark policy: integration-owned `main`, design or feature work on the
    38	named long-lived or task branch, and integration only after producer refs are
    39	available for consumers.
    40	
    41	For main feature integration, start from current `main`, work on a named
    42	integration bookmark while the feature is not green, test the affected branch
    43	family together, rebase on moved `main` before landing, then land producers
    44	before consumers. Remove temporary local path overrides before the merge-ready
    45	state unless the branch dependency is intentional and documented.
    46	If the work creates or consumes a producer dependency, make that dependency
    47	portable before publishing. Surface stale dependency pins, unmerged producer
    48	branches, and dependencies that have unmerged branches when they affect
    49	integration, deployment, repurpose, or closeout. If portable closeout is not
    50	possible, report it as a hard blocker.
    51	
    52	If a local repository or worktree is already claimed, do not share it. Create an
    53	isolated main-based feature worktree or workspace, claim that path, and file a
    54	tracker item naming the repository, branch, worktree, and needed final
    55	disposition: discard, partial merge, or full merge.
    56	
    57	## Operation Work Tracking
    58	
    59	Use tracked work items when work must survive the session or coordinate
    60	with other work. Before working an item, inspect its state and dependencies, then
    61	claim only the item actively being worked.
    62	
    63	Create executable item text: desired outcome, owning repository or component,
    64	likely files or surfaces, acceptance criteria, dependencies, blockers, and
    65	expected verification. Wire producer-before-consumer dependencies explicitly.
    66	
    67	Close an item only after the acceptance criteria pass or the item is explicitly
    68	invalidated. Closing notes name durable evidence: commit, output file,
    69	validation artifact, or superseding task. If blocked, leave it open and name
    70	the blocker.
    71	
    72	## Operation Push And Closeout
    73	
    74	Before pushing, confirm bookmark reachability, repository status, and that no
    75	descriptionless authored commit is being published. Push the intended bookmark
    76	and return the result.
    77	
    78	After pushing, verify status is clean or contains only named unrelated files.
    79	Report basis commit, branch bookmark, temporary overrides used for testing,
    80	commands run, push result, and any remaining disposition or follow-up.
    20	`AGENTS.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md` when architecture or psyche-stated project
    21	direction is already known, `IDEAS.md` when speculative future projects must be
    22	kept out of accepted architecture and active tracking, `NON_IDEAL_AGENTS.md`
    23	when required workaround debt must guide agents, and repo-local `skills.md` when
    24	the repo has specific working rules, build metadata, source layout, and test
    25	entry points. Durable project direction lands in `ARCHITECTURE.md` (or a code
    26	stub with an explanatory comment), never a per-repo `INTENT.md`.
    27	
    28	Do not invent product behavior, public APIs, storage schemas, deployment
    29	promises, or role authority. Leave TODOs only for real downstream work that the
    30	brief accepts.
    31	
    32	## Scaffold Language Fit
    33	
    34	Prefer the ecosystem already implied by the repo or brief. Rust scaffolds follow
    35	the workspace Rust shape. Nix scaffolds expose checks through the flake rather
    36	than ad hoc scripts. Names use full English words unless the surrounding
    37	ecosystem has a canonical exception.
    38	
    39	## Scaffold Handoff
    40	
    41	Run the narrow scaffold check available: parser check, formatter, flake
    42	evaluation, test discovery, or generated-output check. If the scaffold is
    43	intentionally incomplete, name the missing piece and the first command expected
    44	to pass once it exists.
     1	# Role - repo scaffolder
     2	
     3	## Contract
     4	
     5	The Repo Scaffolder prepares a new repository or significant structural rework
     6	from accepted intent and local conventions. It builds the starting shape so an
     7	implementer can work inside it; it does not smuggle in product behavior beyond
     8	the scaffold brief.
     9	
    10	## Workflow
    11	
    12	Read the workspace and repo-local guidance before editing. If the work creates a
    13	new repo, establish the expected guidance surfaces, build metadata, source
    14	layout, test entry points, and minimal documentation required by the ecosystem.
    15	If the work reshapes an existing repo, preserve existing ownership boundaries
    16	and migrate only the structure named by the brief.
    17	
    18	Prefer the repository's current language, build system, schema system, and
    19	module conventions. For Rust work, keep examples and source layout consistent
    20	with workspace Rust discipline. For Nix work, expose checks through the flake
    21	rather than ad hoc shell scripts.
    22	
    23	## Boundaries
    24	
    25	Do not invent product features, public APIs, storage schemas, deployment
    26	promises, or role authority. Leave implementation TODOs only when they identify
    27	real downstream work.
    28	
    29	## Verification
    30	
    31	Run the narrow scaffold checks available in the repo: formatting, parser checks,
    32	flake evaluation, or test discovery as appropriate. If a check cannot run
    33	because the scaffold is intentionally incomplete, state the exact missing piece.
    34	
    35	## Output
    36	
    37	Return the scaffold handoff in chat or the harness-required worker output. Write
    38	an output artifact only when the brief requests a downstream pickup file; then
    39	use the requested path or the opt-in artifact naming protocol.
    45	order and bare atoms for canonical strings.
    46	
    47	The active output manifest lists emitted outputs. The dependency index maps
    48	module identifiers to source paths and dependency module identifiers. Assemble
    49	role packets from the active manifest and dependency index.
    50	
    51	## Skill Source Reconciliation
    52	
    53	After source edits, run the generator or check command when available. Treat a
    54	skill edit as deployed only when the generated runtime surfaces active agents
    55	read are reconciled; stale generated outputs are a deployment gap. Confirm that
    56	every manifest or dependency path exists, new headings are unique within their
    57	source file, generated runtime outputs would not receive provenance notices, and
    58	role packets include the doctrine the manifest names without pulling the whole
    59	corpus.
    60	
    61	Dirty consuming workspaces do not block generation or required whole-working-copy
    62	commits. Only direct ownership of a required generated path blocks deployment;
    63	name the owner and path precisely.
    64	
    65	After generation and checks pass, close out source edits with commit and push.
    66	Name unrelated working-copy changes or included peer changes according to repo
    67	doctrine. In the result, suggest further removals or rewordings that could
    68	reduce bloat or improve clarity but were not safe or in scope for the pass.

    43	CriomOS services are NixOS modules or typed systemd services, directly on a host
    44	or inside a contained node. Prefer declarative modules, package upstream sources
    45	natively, and make secrets, state, users, ports, and checks visible in the Nix
    46	shape.
    47	
    48	Runtime compilation caches, generated configs, and build artifacts that affect
    49	startup behavior belong in derivations when feasible, so rebuilds are
    50	repeatable and cold starts do not depend on mutable user state.
    51	
    52	## Nix Commands
    53	
    54	Use Nix commands that prove the surface directly: `nix eval`, `nix flake show`,
    55	`nix path-info`, `nix build`, `nix run`, and `nix flake check`. Do not search
    56	the Nix store filesystem. Do not record raw store paths in durable prose as the
    57	proof of correctness.
    58	
    59	## Nix Tests And Safety
    60	
    61	Expose durable checks as flake checks, named apps, packages, or scripts entered
    62	through Nix. Stateful checks name their state directory and leave inspectable
    63	artifacts. VM or live-host checks run only when the host is authorized for that
    64	class of work.
    65	
    66	Keep secrets transient. Do not put secret values in Nix store paths, logs,
    67	reports, chat, commits, or generated outputs.
     1	# Skill — main feature integration
     2	
     3	Use this when updating a code repo's `main` with a feature that is not already
     4	green. This applies to code repos under `/git`, not primary.
     5	
     6	## Flow
     7	
     8	1. Start from the current `main` head: `jj new main`.
     9	2. Work on a named integration bookmark while the feature is not green.
    10	3. Keep each affected repo on a main-based branch; do not stack new work on stale
    11	   peer integration branches unless the task is explicitly to integrate that
    12	   branch.
    13	4. Test the branch family together. Temporary local path overrides are allowed
    14	   during testing, but remove them from the merge-ready state unless the branch
    15	   dependency is intentional and documented.
    16	5. When the candidate is green, fetch/recheck main. If main moved, rebase the
    17	   branch onto the new main and rerun the relevant tests.
    18	6. Land main in dependency order. For a compiler stack, land producers before
    19	   consumers, then update consumer lockfiles against the newly landed main.
    20	7. Push main only after the post-rebase checks are green.
    21	
    22	## Reporting
    23	
    24	State the basis commit, the branch bookmark, the exact temporary overrides used
    25	for testing, the tests run, and whether final main landing happened or remains
    26	blocked on an upstream branch landing.
    35	
    36	Code repositories keep one logical change per commit. Follow the repo's branch
    37	or bookmark policy: integration-owned `main`, design or feature work on the
    38	named long-lived or task branch, and integration only after producer refs are
    39	available for consumers.
    40	
    41	For main feature integration, start from current `main`, work on a named
    42	integration bookmark while the feature is not green, test the affected branch
    43	family together, rebase on moved `main` before landing, then land producers
    44	before consumers. Remove temporary local path overrides before the merge-ready
    45	state unless the branch dependency is intentional and documented.
    46	If the work creates or consumes a producer dependency, make that dependency
    47	portable before publishing. Surface stale dependency pins, unmerged producer
    48	branches, and dependencies that have unmerged branches when they affect
    49	integration, deployment, repurpose, or closeout. If portable closeout is not
    50	possible, report it as a hard blocker.
    51	
    52	If a local repository or worktree is already claimed, do not share it. Create an
    53	isolated main-based feature worktree or workspace, claim that path, and file a
    54	tracker item naming the repository, branch, worktree, and needed final
    55	disposition: discard, partial merge, or full merge.
    56	
    57	## Operation Work Tracking
    58	
    59	Use tracked work items when work must survive the session or coordinate
    60	with other work. Before working an item, inspect its state and dependencies, then
    61	claim only the item actively being worked.
    62	
    63	Create executable item text: desired outcome, owning repository or component,
    64	likely files or surfaces, acceptance criteria, dependencies, blockers, and
    65	expected verification. Wire producer-before-consumer dependencies explicitly.
    66	
    67	Close an item only after the acceptance criteria pass or the item is explicitly
    68	invalidated. Closing notes name durable evidence: commit, output file,
    69	validation artifact, or superseding task. If blocked, leave it open and name
    70	the blocker.
    71	
    72	## Operation Push And Closeout
    73	
    74	Before pushing, confirm bookmark reachability, repository status, and that no
    75	descriptionless authored commit is being published. Push the intended bookmark
    76	and return the result.
    77	
    78	After pushing, verify status is clean or contains only named unrelated files.
    79	Report basis commit, branch bookmark, temporary overrides used for testing,
    80	commands run, push result, and any remaining disposition or follow-up.
    20	`AGENTS.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md` when architecture or psyche-stated project
    21	direction is already known, `IDEAS.md` when speculative future projects must be
    22	kept out of accepted architecture and active tracking, `NON_IDEAL_AGENTS.md`
    23	when required workaround debt must guide agents, and repo-local `skills.md` when
    24	the repo has specific working rules, build metadata, source layout, and test
    25	entry points. Durable project direction lands in `ARCHITECTURE.md` (or a code
    26	stub with an explanatory comment), never a per-repo `INTENT.md`.
    27	
    28	Do not invent product behavior, public APIs, storage schemas, deployment
    29	promises, or role authority. Leave TODOs only for real downstream work that the
    30	brief accepts.
    31	
    32	## Scaffold Language Fit
    33	
    34	Prefer the ecosystem already implied by the repo or brief. Rust scaffolds follow
    35	the workspace Rust shape. Nix scaffolds expose checks through the flake rather
    36	than ad hoc scripts. Names use full English words unless the surrounding
    37	ecosystem has a canonical exception.
    38	
    39	## Scaffold Handoff
    40	
    41	Run the narrow scaffold check available: parser check, formatter, flake
    42	evaluation, test discovery, or generated-output check. If the scaffold is
    43	intentionally incomplete, name the missing piece and the first command expected
    44	to pass once it exists.
     1	# Role - repo scaffolder
     2	
     3	## Contract
     4	
     5	The Repo Scaffolder prepares a new repository or significant structural rework
     6	from accepted intent and local conventions. It builds the starting shape so an
     7	implementer can work inside it; it does not smuggle in product behavior beyond
     8	the scaffold brief.
     9	
    10	## Workflow
    11	
    12	Read the workspace and repo-local guidance before editing. If the work creates a
    13	new repo, establish the expected guidance surfaces, build metadata, source
    14	layout, test entry points, and minimal documentation required by the ecosystem.
    15	If the work reshapes an existing repo, preserve existing ownership boundaries
    16	and migrate only the structure named by the brief.
    17	
    18	Prefer the repository's current language, build system, schema system, and
    19	module conventions. For Rust work, keep examples and source layout consistent
    20	with workspace Rust discipline. For Nix work, expose checks through the flake
    21	rather than ad hoc shell scripts.
    22	
    23	## Boundaries
    24	
    25	Do not invent product features, public APIs, storage schemas, deployment
    26	promises, or role authority. Leave implementation TODOs only when they identify
    27	real downstream work.
    28	
    29	## Verification
    30	
    31	Run the narrow scaffold checks available in the repo: formatting, parser checks,
    32	flake evaluation, or test discovery as appropriate. If a check cannot run
    33	because the scaffold is intentionally incomplete, state the exact missing piece.
    34	
    35	## Output
    36	
    37	Return the scaffold handoff in chat or the harness-required worker output. Write
    38	an output artifact only when the brief requests a downstream pickup file; then
    39	use the requested path or the opt-in artifact naming protocol.
    45	order and bare atoms for canonical strings.
    46	
    47	The active output manifest lists emitted outputs. The dependency index maps
    48	module identifiers to source paths and dependency module identifiers. Assemble
    49	role packets from the active manifest and dependency index.
    50	
    51	## Skill Source Reconciliation
    52	
    53	After source edits, run the generator or check command when available. Treat a
    54	skill edit as deployed only when the generated runtime surfaces active agents
    55	read are reconciled; stale generated outputs are a deployment gap. Confirm that
    56	every manifest or dependency path exists, new headings are unique within their
    57	source file, generated runtime outputs would not receive provenance notices, and
    58	role packets include the doctrine the manifest names without pulling the whole
    59	corpus.
    60	
    61	Dirty consuming workspaces do not block generation or required whole-working-copy
    62	commits. Only direct ownership of a required generated path blocks deployment;
    63	name the owner and path precisely.
    64	
    65	After generation and checks pass, close out source edits with commit and push.
    66	Name unrelated working-copy changes or included peer changes according to repo
    67	doctrine. In the result, suggest further removals or rewordings that could
    68	reduce bloat or improve clarity but were not safe or in scope for the pass.

     3	## Rust Discipline Purpose
     4	
     5	Rust discipline gives code writers and auditors the baseline shape expected in
     6	workspace Rust. It is role composition, not a runtime lookup.

     9

    11	trait implementation. Avoid free helpers except `main` and required test
    12	wrappers.

     1	# Skill — feature development
     2	
     3	## Rules
     4	
     5	Use feature branches or separate worktrees for code-repo feature work, experiments, rewrites, and prototypes that may ship. Do not do feature work directly on a shared integration line unless the repo instructions explicitly say so.
     6

     8	
     9	Base feature work off `main`. If the shared checkout is claimed or already being edited, create an isolated worktree/branch from `main`, claim that worktree path with Orchestrate, and file a bead naming the branch plus final disposition: discard, partial merge, or full merge.

    12	
    13	Different worktrees of the same repository are separate claim scopes; the same worktree is a conflict.

    16	
    17	Subagents that edit code or produce ship-ready prototypes use their assigned feature branch or worktree. Research-only workers that write only their assigned output need no worktree.

## Observations

### Directly conflicting or weakening passages

- `modules/code-implementation/full.md:13`  
  > “Treat bare language tests as inner-loop evidence unless the repository names them as durable gates.”  
  This explicitly permits bare local language tests.

- `modules/code-implementation-core/full.md:52-56`  
  > “durable test evidence is owned by Nix when the repo exposes it…”  
  > “Bare language test commands are inner-loop evidence unless the repo says otherwise.”  
  This weakens the requested universal ban by allowing an exception.

- `roles/general-code-implementer/full.md:33-36`  
  > “Run the narrowest meaningful tests first…”  
  It does not constrain “tests” to Nix and therefore can lead to `cargo test`.

- `modules/versioning/full.md:26-28`  
  > “run the relevant tests”  
  Unqualified test execution is prescribed during closeout.

- `modules/main-feature-integration/full.md:13-17,24-25`  
  > “Test the branch family together…”  
  > “rerun the relevant tests.”  
  Again, no Nix-only qualification.

- `roles/rust-auditor/full.md:30-32`  
  > “Run or inspect the Rust checks named by the implementer.”  
  “Checks” is safer than “tests,” but the role does not prohibit accepting Cargo evidence.

- `roles/repo-scaffolder/full.md:31-33` and `modules/repo-scaffold-core/full.md:41-44`  
  Both allow “test discovery”/“test entry” checks without specifying Nix.

- `modules/nix-usage/full.md:7`  
  > “Use local checkout evaluations for diagnosis…”  
  This is Nix-related, not Cargo-specific, but should be distinguished from valid remote test evidence.

### Already aligned

- `modules/testing/full.md:7-15` establishes Nix as the normal test gate and requires flake checks.
- `modules/nix-core/full.md:16-18,52-64` requires portable/remote Nix surfaces and named checks/apps/scripts.
- `modules/nix-usage/full.md:34-40` provides the existing remote-proof mechanism: `--option max-jobs 0`, uncached derivation or `--rebuild`, and explicit evidence classification.
- `modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:39-45,65-67` documents the remote Prometheus path and builder reachability.

No `roles/reviewer/full.md` exists in the authoritative repository; the requested reviewer packet has no source file to amend.

### Rust-specific sources

`modules/rust-core/full.md:31-36`, `modules/rust-crate-layout/full.md:15-21,58-84`, and `modules/rust-discipline/full.md:8-19` describe test structure and architecture but do not prescribe local Cargo execution. They need only a shared testing-reference amendment if desired, not repetitive patches.

## Generated reach

- `.agents/skills/testing/SKILL.md`, `.agents/skills/code-implementation/SKILL.md`, etc. are generated projections of `modules/*/full.md`.
- `.pi/agents/<role>.md` role packets include the role source plus universal and configured modules. For example, `.pi/agents/general-code-implementer.md:200-206` contains the generated `code-implementation-core` wording.
- Generator ownership is in `src/assembly.rs`, especially role module assembly around `assembled_modules` and target paths around lines `1431-1463`.
- Therefore amendments belong in authoritative `modules/*`, `roles/*`, and possibly manifests only if module inclusion changes. Do not patch `.agents` or `.pi`.

## Minimal amendment set

1. Amend `modules/testing/full.md` with the normative universal rule and remote-builder evidence procedure.
2. Amend `modules/nix-usage/full.md` to make remote execution proof mandatory for test evidence, reusing `max-jobs 0` and builder-log guidance.
3. Replace the exception in `modules/code-implementation-core/full.md:52-56` and its duplicate standalone wording in `modules/code-implementation/full.md`.
4. Qualify generic verification wording in:
   - `roles/general-code-implementer/full.md`
   - `modules/versioning/full.md`
   - `modules/main-feature-integration/full.md`
   - `roles/rust-auditor/full.md`
   - `roles/nix-auditor/full.md`
   - `roles/operating-system-implementer/full.md`
   - scaffolding/closeout wording where “tests” is intended as execution.
5. Leave Rust layout/architecture modules unchanged unless the psyche wants all Rust test references explicitly cross-linked.

## Proposed normative wording

> Bare local language test commands, including `cargo test`, are invalid testing evidence and must not be run for testing. Test suites must be exposed and executed through Nix as flake checks or named Nix test outputs. Testing evidence is valid only when the Nix derivation executed on an authorized remote builder, not via local fallback.

> To prove remote execution, inspect daemon builder configuration with `nix config show`, run the named test derivation with local slots disabled (`--option max-jobs 0`), use `--print-build-logs`, and retain output showing the remote builder or an equivalent builder-attribution record. A cached substitute is not proof of test execution; use an uncached derivation or `--rebuild` when necessary.

## Scope questions for psyche discussion

- Does the ban cover only test execution, or also `cargo check`, Clippy, rustdoc/doctests, benchmarks, and formatting?
- Must every stateful test be a named Nix output, or may an explicitly documented Nix-entered runner qualify?
- What exact remote-builder attribution is authoritative?
- If remote builders are unavailable, should agents stop and report blocked, or allow a clearly labeled non-evidence diagnostic?
- Should existing repos lacking Nix test exposure receive a migration task before implementation proceeds?

## Migration risk and transition

Repos without Nix-exposed tests cannot immediately produce compliant evidence. Transitional behavior should be:

1. Do not run `cargo test`.
2. Report the missing Nix check/output as a blocker.
3. Add or queue the smallest Nix test exposure needed.
4. Permit only non-test diagnostics (for example source inspection or Nix evaluation) and never silently fall back to Cargo.

```acceptance-report
{
  "criteriaSatisfied": [
    {
      "id": "criterion-1",
      "status": "satisfied",
      "evidence": "Read-only doctrine audit completed; no files changed and no builds/tests invoked."
    },
    {
      "id": "criterion-2",
      "status": "satisfied",
      "evidence": "Authoritative source paths, line-located conflicting passages, generator reach, amendment set, wording, verification procedure, scope questions, and migration risks are listed."
    }
  ],
  "changedFiles": [],
  "testsAddedOrUpdated": [],

## Observations

### Directly conflicting or weakening passages

- `modules/code-implementation/full.md:13`  
  > “Treat bare language tests as inner-loop evidence unless the repository names them as durable gates.”  
  This explicitly permits bare local language tests.

- `modules/code-implementation-core/full.md:52-56`  
  > “durable test evidence is owned by Nix when the repo exposes it…”  
  > “Bare language test commands are inner-loop evidence unless the repo says otherwise.”  
  This weakens the requested universal ban by allowing an exception.

- `roles/general-code-implementer/full.md:33-36`  
  > “Run the narrowest meaningful tests first…”  
  It does not constrain “tests” to Nix and therefore can lead to `cargo test`.

- `modules/versioning/full.md:26-28`  
  > “run the relevant tests”  
  Unqualified test execution is prescribed during closeout.

- `modules/main-feature-integration/full.md:13-17,24-25`  
  > “Test the branch family together…”  
  > “rerun the relevant tests.”  
  Again, no Nix-only qualification.

- `roles/rust-auditor/full.md:30-32`  
  > “Run or inspect the Rust checks named by the implementer.”  
  “Checks” is safer than “tests,” but the role does not prohibit accepting Cargo evidence.

- `roles/repo-scaffolder/full.md:31-33` and `modules/repo-scaffold-core/full.md:41-44`  
  Both allow “test discovery”/“test entry” checks without specifying Nix.

- `modules/nix-usage/full.md:7`  
  > “Use local checkout evaluations for diagnosis…”  
  This is Nix-related, not Cargo-specific, but should be distinguished from valid remote test evidence.

### Already aligned

- `modules/testing/full.md:7-15` establishes Nix as the normal test gate and requires flake checks.
- `modules/nix-core/full.md:16-18,52-64` requires portable/remote Nix surfaces and named checks/apps/scripts.
- `modules/nix-usage/full.md:34-40` provides the existing remote-proof mechanism: `--option max-jobs 0`, uncached derivation or `--rebuild`, and explicit evidence classification.
- `modules/nixos-vm-testing/full.md:39-45,65-67` documents the remote Prometheus path and builder reachability.

No `roles/reviewer/full.md` exists in the authoritative repository; the requested reviewer packet has no source file to amend.

### Rust-specific sources

`modules/rust-core/full.md:31-36`, `modules/rust-crate-layout/full.md:15-21,58-84`, and `modules/rust-discipline/full.md:8-19` describe test structure and architecture but do not prescribe local Cargo execution. They need only a shared testing-reference amendment if desired, not repetitive patches.

## Generated reach

- `.agents/skills/testing/SKILL.md`, `.agents/skills/code-implementation/SKILL.md`, etc. are generated projections of `modules/*/full.md`.
- `.pi/agents/<role>.md` role packets include the role source plus universal and configured modules. For example, `.pi/agents/general-code-implementer.md:200-206` contains the generated `code-implementation-core` wording.
- Generator ownership is in `src/assembly.rs`, especially role module assembly around `assembled_modules` and target paths around lines `1431-1463`.
- Therefore amendments belong in authoritative `modules/*`, `roles/*`, and possibly manifests only if module inclusion changes. Do not patch `.agents` or `.pi`.

## Minimal amendment set

1. Amend `modules/testing/full.md` with the normative universal rule and remote-builder evidence procedure.
2. Amend `modules/nix-usage/full.md` to make remote execution proof mandatory for test evidence, reusing `max-jobs 0` and builder-log guidance.
3. Replace the exception in `modules/code-implementation-core/full.md:52-56` and its duplicate standalone wording in `modules/code-implementation/full.md`.
4. Qualify generic verification wording in:
   - `roles/general-code-implementer/full.md`
   - `modules/versioning/full.md`
   - `modules/main-feature-integration/full.md`
   - `roles/rust-auditor/full.md`
   - `roles/nix-auditor/full.md`
   - `roles/operating-system-implementer/full.md`
   - scaffolding/closeout wording where “tests” is intended as execution.
5. Leave Rust layout/architecture modules unchanged unless the psyche wants all Rust test references explicitly cross-linked.

## Proposed normative wording

> Bare local language test commands, including `cargo test`, are invalid testing evidence and must not be run for testing. Test suites must be exposed and executed through Nix as flake checks or named Nix test outputs. Testing evidence is valid only when the Nix derivation executed on an authorized remote builder, not via local fallback.

> To prove remote execution, inspect daemon builder configuration with `nix config show`, run the named test derivation with local slots disabled (`--option max-jobs 0`), use `--print-build-logs`, and retain output showing the remote builder or an equivalent builder-attribution record. A cached substitute is not proof of test execution; use an uncached derivation or `--rebuild` when necessary.

## Scope questions for psyche discussion

- Does the ban cover only test execution, or also `cargo check`, Clippy, rustdoc/doctests, benchmarks, and formatting?
- Must every stateful test be a named Nix output, or may an explicitly documented Nix-entered runner qualify?
- What exact remote-builder attribution is authoritative?
- If remote builders are unavailable, should agents stop and report blocked, or allow a clearly labeled non-evidence diagnostic?
- Should existing repos lacking Nix test exposure receive a migration task before implementation proceeds?

## Migration risk and transition

Repos without Nix-exposed tests cannot immediately produce compliant evidence. Transitional behavior should be:

1. Do not run `cargo test`.
2. Report the missing Nix check/output as a blocker.
3. Add or queue the smallest Nix test exposure needed.
4. Permit only non-test diagnostics (for example source inspection or Nix evaluation) and never silently fall back to Cargo.
